glossary.definition: systems that are developed based on diverse interpretations of artificial intelligence
glossary.sentence: On the other hand, the diversity of interpretations of the concept of artificial intelligence permits a variety of intelligent computer systems and research works to be developed.

glossary.definition: systems based on the approach of rational agents
glossary.sentence: Systems acting rationally are based on the approach of rational agents .

glossary.definition: agents that act to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome
glossary.sentence: A rational agent is one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.

glossary.definition: any entity which is capable to perceive its environment and to act within it
glossary.sentence: In this context, an agent is any entity which is capable to perceive its environment and to act within it.

glossary.definition: a fundamental test in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence
glossary.sentence: The Turing test underlies systems that act humanly and it is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence.

glossary.definition: a person in the third room who does not see nor artificial intelligence, nor human being and asks questions to both intelligences using a text-based input tool
glossary.sentence: In the third room there is a tester (Fig.5). The tester does not see nor artificial intelligence, nor human being and asks questions to both intelligences using a text-based input tool.

glossary.definition: a field of artificial intelligence that developed from studies of how language is related to thinking
glossary.sentence: Studies of how language is related to thinking contributed to natural language processing and knowledge representation in the field of artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: a field of artificial intelligence that developed from studies of how language is related to thinking
glossary.sentence: Studies of how language is related to thinking contributed to natural language processing and knowledge representation in the field of artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: a human mechanism used to store all previously acquired knowledge
glossary.sentence: Humans use for this purpose long-term memory to store all previously acquired knowledge and short-term memory - to store knowledge processed at a specific time;

glossary.definition: a human mechanism used to store knowledge processed at a specific time
glossary.sentence: Humans use for this purpose long-term memory to store all previously acquired knowledge and short-term memory - to store knowledge processed at a specific time;

glossary.definition: a capability required to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions
glossary.sentence: automated reasoning, in order to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions;

glossary.definition: a capability required to be able to adapt to a new situation, as well as to acquire and generalize patterns of different situations
glossary.sentence: machine learning, in order to be able to adapt to a new situation, as well as to acquire and generalize patterns of different situations (for example, to a new topic or intent of the tester, etc.).

glossary.definition: a version of the Turing test that brings forward two more requirements: the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: a capability required for the computer to perceive objects
glossary.sentence: the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: a capability required for the computer to manipulate objects and move about
glossary.sentence: the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: a concept that is still difficult to define due to the richness of manifestations of intelligence performance in our world
glossary.sentence: So mastering this topic you found out that there is a richness of manifestations of intelligence performance in our world, and, as a result, it is still difficult to define what intelligence is.

glossary.definition: a field that is considered a part of the broader field of computer science
glossary.sentence: artificial intelligence is a field that is considered a part of the broader field of computer science

glossary.definition: a field that develops and offers a variety of solutions such as gaming software, self-driving cars, industrial robots and many others
glossary.sentence: This enables the industry of artificial intelligence to develop and offer a variety of solutions – gaming software, self-driving cars, industrial robots and many others.

glossary.definition: a scientific field that made possible artificial intelligence by considering an idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge
glossary.sentence: Philosophy - Philosophers made possible artificial intelligence by considering an idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge.

glossary.definition: a scientific field that provided mathematical formalization in computing, logics, and probability theory
glossary.sentence: Mathematics - The transition from the philosophy to the formal science required mathematical formalization in three main areas: computing, logics, and probability theory.

glossary.definition: a scientific field that offered theories on how to make choices that lead to the desired results and laid the grounds for studies of rational agents
glossary.sentence: Economics - It offered theories on how to make choices that lead to the desired results and laid the grounds for studies of rational agents.

glossary.definition: a scientific field whose contributions to artificial intelligence are related to research on how the human brain processes information
glossary.sentence: Neuroscience – Contributions of neuroscience in artificial intelligence is related to research on how the human brain processes information.

glossary.definition: simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain, and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks are simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain, and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections.

glossary.definition: a scientific field that reinforced the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines
glossary.sentence: Psychology - Psychologists reinforced the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines.

glossary.definition: a scientific field that provided artefacts which made possible development of artificial intelligence applications
glossary.sentence: Computer engineering – Computer engineering provided artefacts, which made possible development of artificial intelligence applications.

glossary.definition: a scientific field whose main contribution to artificial intelligence is related to the feedback theory
glossary.sentence: Control science and cybernetics - The main contribution of this field to artificial intelligence is related to the feedback theory, which offered an idea that all intelligent behaviour is the result of feedback mechanism, that is, a mechanism which can be simulated also by the computer.

glossary.definition: a scientific field that showed that the use of language fits the model of psychologists that humans and animals can be considered as data processing machines
glossary.sentence: Linguistics - Linguists showed that the use of language fits the model of psychologists that humans and animals can be considered as data processing machines.

glossary.definition: a goal related to understanding of those principles which make possible intelligent behaviour
glossary.sentence: The industry of artificial intelligence enables the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the .

glossary.definition: models intellectual behaviour using explicit symbolic representations of problems and their solutions and rules for transforming these symbols
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence models intellectual behaviour using explicit symbolic representations of problems and their solutions and rules for transforming these symbols.

glossary.definition: models intellectual behaviour based on distributed representations, learning from data and pattern recognition, without relying on explicit rules and symbolic structures
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence models intellectual behaviour based on distributed representations, learning from data and pattern recognition, without relying on explicit rules and symbolic structures.

glossary.definition: a hypothesis defined by Newell and Simon in 1976 that states: 'A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action'
glossary.sentence: It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976)

glossary.definition: a physical system capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols
glossary.sentence: A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: meaningful physical patterns that could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality
glossary.sentence: symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality

glossary.definition: symbols that are connected to each other in some way, depicting important aspects of a problem domain
glossary.sentence: symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain

glossary.definition: constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying, which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system
glossary.sentence: operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: essentially involves transforming physical symbols according to rules
glossary.sentence: Summarising all of the above, it can be said that " all intelligent behavior essentially involves transforming physical symbols according to rules " (Bermúdez, 2014).

glossary.definition: the way intelligent computer systems based on symbolic artificial intelligence represent problem domain knowledge (objects and their relations) through a set of symbols
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems based on this paradigm therefore explicitly represent problem domain knowledge (objects and their relations) through a set of symbols (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: knowledge consisting mainly of generalisations that are available in the relevant problem domain
glossary.sentence: This knowledge consists mainly of generalisations that are available in the relevant problem domain.

glossary.definition: a physical symbol system that uses symbols 0 and 1, combined into strings to represent objects in the world, such as the digit "3" represented by the string "00110011", and creates and operates on these structures using basic machine language routines and higher-level programming languages
glossary.sentence: The computer uses symbols 0 and 1. These symbols are combined into strings of 0 and 1 to represent objects in the world, e.g. the digit "3" is represented by the string "00110011". These strings are symbolic structures. The computer creates these structures and operates on them using basic machine language routines. At the same time, computers also execute programs written in higher-level programming languages. These programmes use different symbols and symbolic structures to invoke basic procedures.

glossary.definition: a criticism of the physical symbol system hypothesis described by John Searle, in which a person who does not understand Chinese manipulates Chinese symbols according to an instruction manual in English, producing correct outputs without understanding, demonstrating that symbol manipulation alone does not imply intelligence
glossary.sentence: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism. Searle describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence. The idea of the argument is the following. Imagine a room in which there is a person who receives an input through one slot and produces an output through another slot. Both input and output are represented as a piece of paper with Chinese symbols written on it. The person does not know Chinese and cannot understand what is written. He/she uses an instruction manual written in English that tells the person in the room how to respond to the paper received. Therefore, the only thing the person does is to match the Chinese symbols received with symbols presented in the instruction manual in some sort of formal way – according to their shape, for example. The matching operation allows him/her to identify which symbols to produce as an output. Therefore, the person does not understand the written symbols but is able to provide the right output. To someone outside the room it might look as if there is intelligent behaviour going on. In reality, there is no intelligence.

glossary.definition: a way of representing knowledge in connectionist artificial intelligence as a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units, rather than in physical symbols
glossary.sentence: knowledge is not directly represented in the system because it is considered as a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units, rather than a quantity encoded in physical symbols (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: in connectionist artificial intelligence, knowledge is encoded as numerical values in the weights of the connections, which are changed during the operation of the network
glossary.sentence: In connectionist artificial intelligence, knowledge is encoded as numerical values in the weights of the connections, which are changed during the operation of the network.

glossary.definition: identifying the structure or pattern in data
glossary.sentence: pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data

glossary.definition: the relationship between symbolic and connectionist artificial intelligence paradigms, which are not competing but rather support each other, as seen in the AlphaGo program where tree-based search complements artificial neural networks
glossary.sentence: It is important to note that the connectionist and symbolic artificial intelligence paradigms of thinking in contemporary artificial intelligence are complementary rather than competing (Russell and Norvig, 2020), for example, tree-based search complements artificial neural networks in the AlphaGo program, which is designed to play the board game Go (Goel, 2021).

glossary.definition: a sequence of actions and decisions aimed at achieving a goal, as described in the context of playing a game.
glossary.sentence: Playing a game can be seen as a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: a game in which the initial state is a blank grid, and players aim to reach a goal state with three marks in a horizontal, vertical, or diagonal row.
glossary.sentence: Let's take the game of tic-tac-toe as an example.

glossary.definition: the starting condition of a game, represented by a blank grid when the game begins.
glossary.sentence: In this game, the initial state of the problem-solving process is a blank grid, which appears when you start the game (Figure 3).

glossary.definition: a state in which three marks corresponding to a player are located in a horizontal, vertical, or diagonal row.
glossary.sentence: Each player wants to reach one of the goal states, in which three marks corresponding to that player are located in a horizontal, vertical or a diagonal row (Figure 4).

glossary.definition: an action taken by a player to place their mark in an empty square on the grid.
glossary.sentence: To reach a goal state, the players make moves by putting their marks in empty squares.

glossary.definition: a state that results from a move, changing the appearance of the game grid.
glossary.sentence: Each mark added to the game grid changes the appearance of the grid. Each of the possible moves produces a specific intermediate state.

glossary.definition: A graph representing all possible states in a search problem, where nodes are states and edges are transitions between states.
glossary.sentence: Let us consider an example of Best-First Search by implementing data-driven search on the state space graph that you can see in Figure 8.

glossary.definition: a discrete state of the game represented in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Its nodes correspond to discrete states of the game, and arcs are used to represent legal moves in the game.

glossary.definition: a connection between nodes in the state space graph that represents a legal move in the game.
glossary.sentence: Its nodes correspond to discrete states of the game, and arcs are used to represent legal moves in the game.

glossary.term: deterministic two-player game of perfect information
glossary.definition: a game where moves are made alternately, and both players have full knowledge of the game state at all times, with no randomness.
glossary.sentence: Since moves are made alternately in a deterministic two-player game of perfect information, node levels are clearly distinguishable in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: a layer in the state space graph that contains states from which only one of the two players can make a move.
glossary.sentence: One or more nodes are located at each level, and these display states of the game from which only one of the two players can make a move.

glossary.definition: a move that follows the rules of the game and is allowed at a given state.
glossary.sentence: Each of the possible moves produces a specific intermediate state.

glossary.definition: a sequence in which players take turns making moves in the game.
glossary.sentence: Since moves are made alternately in a deterministic two-player game of perfect information, node levels are clearly distinguishable in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: the rule that arcs must only connect nodes between adjacent levels, not within the same level or across multiple levels.
glossary.sentence: We must avoid using any arcs in the state space graph other than those between two adjacent levels, and especially arcs between nodes at the same level or arcs crossing several levels (Figure 7).

glossary.term: zero-sum deterministic two-player game of perfect information
glossary.definition: a game type where one player's gain is exactly balanced by the other's loss, with no randomness and full information available to both players.
glossary.sentence: If an arc was drawn between two nodes at the same level, this would indicate that the same player could make two moves rather than only one, which would contradict the principle of a zero-sum deterministic two-player game of perfect information as defined at the beginning of this topic.

glossary.definition: a system capable of using a state space graph to play a game effectively by following formal rules and algorithms.
glossary.sentence: An intelligent computer system will effectively be able to use the state space graph for the game only if each node in the graph clearly represents a game state at any specific moment in time.

glossary.definition: features related to a computer implementation of a game, corresponding to the properties of the game and its course, which change after a move and are represented in each node of the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Before creating a state space graph, the developer of an intelligent computer system should determine the parameters that characterise the states in the game. These parameters can be described as follows: they are related to a computer implementation of a game; they correspond to the properties of the game and its course, in which the values change after making a move during the course of the game; they are not related to the characteristics of the state space graph, such as the number of nodes, the number of levels, the number of arcs, etc.; the values of the parameters allow the players to track the course of the game, since they determine a specific state in the game at a specific moment in time; the values of the parameters should be represented in each node of the state space graph; the parameters depend on the game, and thus may be very different; for example, they may include the score of each player, the current game board, or both the scores of the players and the current game board, etc.

glossary.term: complexity of a state space graph
glossary.definition: a critical aspect that must be reduced to avoid overconsumption of computational resources.
glossary.sentence: The complexity of a state space graph is a critical aspect. It is vital to reduce the complexity to avoid overconsumption of computational resources.

glossary.definition: nodes at the same level that have the same values for all parameters that characterise the states.
glossary.sentence: Nodes at the same level are identical if they have the same values for all of the parameters that characterise the states of the game.

glossary.definition: a terminal node in the game tree where no further moves are possible.
glossary.sentence: It reaches a leaf node in which the minimiser has the highest score and therefore wins the game.

glossary.definition: a way of organizing and storing data, as represented by the state space graph in the context of game playing.
glossary.sentence: The state space graph of the game also represents a data structure.

glossary.definition: a search algorithm that processes the data structure to find a solution to the problem based on known input data
glossary.sentence: A task of any search algorithm is to find a solution path if such a path exists in the state space graph (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: the process of exploring a state space graph to find a solution to a problem, requiring a search algorithm to select the optimal path.
glossary.sentence: Topic 2.3 - State Space Search

glossary.definition: a game with a numerical string and alternating scores, where players remove numbers and subtract them from their scores until one number remains.
glossary.sentence: Let’s consider the example of the construction of a state space graph for the following oversimplified game. A numerical string “2-2-2-3-3” is given at the beginning of the game. The alternate score of the players is 8:8, and the players make alternate moves. During a move, a player removes any number from the numerical string and subtracts it from his or her initial score. The game stops when only one number is left in the numerical string, and the player who has the highest score wins the game. If the score is the same for both players, the result is a draw.

glossary.definition: a sequence of numbers representing a state in the game, such as '2-3'.
glossary.sentence: the numerical string is “2-3”

glossary.definition: a numerical representation of the current game state, such as '3:6'.
glossary.sentence: the score is 3:6

glossary.definition: a game outcome where the player's goal is the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the  the the the the the the the the the the  the the the .
glossary.sentence: If the score is the same for both players, the result is a draw.

glossary.definition: The initial node from which the search process begins.
glossary.sentence: The search start node is A.

glossary.definition: The node that the algorithm aims to find during the search process.
glossary.sentence: The search goal node is J.

glossary.definition: the output of a search algorithm, representing the sequence of nodes from the search start node to the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: They provide a solution path as their output.

glossary.definition: the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated
glossary.sentence: As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated; closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.

glossary.definition: they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated
glossary.sentence: As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated; closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.

glossary.definition: A search strategy that starts from the initial node and proceeds toward the goal node using heuristic information.
glossary.sentence: Let us consider an example of Best-First Search by implementing data-driven search on the state space graph that you can see in Figure 8.

glossary.definition: From a goal back to the data. This is called goal-driven search or backward chaining. The search process starts from a leaf node and it proceeds in backward direction in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: From a goal back to the data. This is called goal-driven search or backward chaining. The search process starts from a leaf node and it proceeds in backward direction in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: The search process starts from a root node and it proceeds in forward direction in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: This is called data-driven search or forward chaining. The search process starts from a root node and it proceeds in forward direction in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: The search process starts from a leaf node and it proceeds in backward direction in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: This is called goal-driven search or backward chaining. The search process starts from a leaf node and it proceeds in backward direction in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: nodes representing initial states of the problem-solving process
glossary.sentence: a state space graph includes nodes representing initial states of the problem-solving process and nodes displaying goal states

glossary.definition: nodes displaying goal states
glossary.sentence: a state space graph includes nodes representing initial states of the problem-solving process and nodes displaying goal states

glossary.definition: The direction in which the search proceeds through the state space graph, which determines whether nodes at the depth limit are considered leaf or root nodes.
glossary.sentence: Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction).

glossary.definition: the process of generating new nodes from the current node by exploring its descendants or ancestors.
glossary.sentence: expanding the current node if it is not the search goal node

glossary.term: measurable/comparable property of the search goal node
glossary.definition: a property used to determine if the search goal node has been reached by comparing the content of the current node and the search goal node
glossary.sentence: Using a measurable/comparable property of the search goal node. In this case, the search algorithm makes a simple comparison between the content of the current node and the search goal node.

glossary.term: checking a property of the solution path
glossary.definition: searching for the shortest path, the longest path, or a path visiting each node only once
glossary.sentence: Checking a property of the solution path, for example, by searching for the shortest path, the longest path, or a path visiting each node only once.

glossary.definition: The path with the minimum number of steps from the search start node to the search goal node, if such a path exists in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: However, this algorithm is not guaranteed to find the shortest path to the search goal node, if such a path exists in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: a path specified based on time, length, resource consumption, and other aspects
glossary.sentence: It should be noted here that the shortest and longest path could be specified based on time, length, resource consumption, and other aspects.

glossary.definition: a system of roads connecting cities, used as an example to demonstrate differences between search algorithms.
glossary.sentence: Imagine that we are building a recommendation system that will advise tourists about the selection of roads between two cities in France: Paris and Toulouse.

glossary.definition: a type of search algorithm that processes only nodes and arcs of the state space graph and does not have additional information about the quality of the nodes.
glossary.sentence: In the case of uninformed search, all of the paths represented in the state space graph are identical, because uninformed search algorithms process only nodes and arcs of the state space graph. They do not have additional information about the quality of the nodes.

glossary.definition: a type of search algorithm that is able to distinguish between paths represented in the state space graph by taking into account factors such as distance, location, road surface, number of traffic lights, and other factors represented as numerical values showing the quality of each node.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms are able to distinguish between paths represented in the state space graph. Therefore, each path differs from all of the others. This is because heuristic search can take into account such information as the distance between any two settlements, the location of each settlement (north, west, etc.) relative to Paris, the surface of the road leading to each settlement, the number of traffic lights between any two settlements, and many other factors. These factors are represented as numerical values showing the quality of each node in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: a data structure that stores a set of elements in sequential order.
glossary.sentence: Search algorithms are often implemented using a data structure called a list. A list stores a set of elements in sequential order.

glossary.definition: A list that stores all of the nodes found so far, where the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7). The priority queue works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.

glossary.definition: A list that stores nodes that have already been expanded.
glossary.sentence: The node A is moved to the CLOSED list as an already expanded node.

glossary.definition: a situation where a node is revisited during the search process, which is avoided by not adding nodes already in OPEN or CLOSED lists.
glossary.sentence: Nodes that are already added to the OPEN or CLOSED lists are not considered again, and they are not added to the lists, with the aim of avoiding cycles and nodes being repeatedly discovered.

glossary.definition: a search method used by the alpha-beta pruning algorithm, in which it starts with the first unconsidered path on the left and goes deep inside the state space graph until it reaches a leaf node or a node with a precise heuristic measure
glossary.sentence: It should be noted that only unconsidered paths are cut off! The algorithm searches the state space graph using a depth-first search (Ertel, 2011), in which it starts with the first unconsidered path on the left and goes deep inside the state space graph until it reaches a leaf node or a node with a precise heuristic measure

glossary.definition: a data structure that works according to the 'Last in, First Out' principle, where the last data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a stack. A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: A single step in the algorithm's execution process, where a specific operation is performed on the current node and the lists OPEN and CLOSED.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 5: The algorithm expands the current node I by generating its three descendants - nodes J, K, and G.

glossary.definition: a node that is below another node in the tree structure, reachable via a path from the parent.
glossary.sentence: the only descendant evaluated so far

glossary.definition: a node in the current path that is above the current node in the tree structure.
glossary.sentence: the ancestor of the current node in the current path

glossary.definition: A step-by-step procedure for solving a problem or performing a task, specifically in the context of searching within a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 5: The algorithm expands the current node I by generating its three descendants - nodes J, K, and G.

glossary.definition: the node currently being processed by the algorithm in the path of traversal.
glossary.sentence: the current node in the current path

glossary.definition: Nodes generated from a current node in the forward direction of the search.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm expands the current node A by generating its three descendants—nodes D, H, and E.

glossary.definition: A problem in Depth-First Search where the algorithm may search for a long time in depth before returning to higher levels, especially in graphs with many node levels.
glossary.sentence: Besides that, the algorithm has another serious shortcoming—the problem of so-called indefinitely deep paths . It means that in a situation where the state space graph has many node levels, Depth-First Search will search for the search goal node for a long time in depth before it returns to higher levels.

glossary.definition: A modification of Depth-First Search that overcomes the drawback of going along indefinitely deep paths by introducing a depth limit.
glossary.sentence: To overcome this shortcoming, a modification of Depth-First Search is offered. Depth-Limited Search is a modification of Depth-First Search that overcomes a drawback of Depth-First Search related to going along indefinitely deep paths.

glossary.definition: A predefined value that defines how deeply the state space graph can be explored; nodes located at this depth are considered leaf/root nodes.
glossary.sentence: The depth limit should be provided before the algorithm starts its operation. Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction).

glossary.definition: Nodes located at the depth limit, which are considered terminal nodes and are not expanded further.
glossary.sentence: Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction).

glossary.definition: A value assigned to a node indicating its depth level, which allows the algorithm to determine whether descendants or ancestors can be generated.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of the algorithm, the depth limit should be defined, and nodes should be added to both lists together with their depth labels. Depth labels allow the algorithm "to understand" if it is possible to generate descendants/ancestors of the current node or if the current node should be considered a leaf/root node.

glossary.definition: A numerical value indicating the level of a node in the state space graph, starting from 0 for the search start node.
glossary.sentence: The depth level of the search start node is equal to 0. The depth level of any other node is calculated by adding 1 to the depth level of the current node.

glossary.definition: To generate the descendants of a node in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 1: The algorithm expands the current node A by generating its three descendants - nodes D, H, and E.

glossary.definition: A search strategy that explores all possible paths in the state space graph to ensure that the search goal node is found if it exists.
glossary.sentence: Depth-First Search is guaranteed to find the search goal node if it exists in the state space graph because this algorithm performs an exhaustive search of the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A specific action performed by the algorithm during each iteration, such as checking if the current node is the goal or if the depth limit is reached.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search includes an additional operational step. In each iteration, the algorithm performs two checks (Figure 12): it checks if the current node is the search goal node and, if not, it checks if the depth of the current node is lower than the specified depth limit.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that always chooses a node whose heuristic measure is higher than the heuristic measure of the current node if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the highest measure, and vice versa—if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the smallest measure, the algorithm will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is less than the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: Hill Climbing has several shortcomings; the most common of these shortcomings are the following (Figure 6): the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction ); the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that selects the best node found so far in each iteration, keeps all of the nodes that were found before, allows switching between different paths in the state space graph, and does not lose a problem solution if the heuristic evaluation function is not well-defined.
glossary.sentence: Best-First Search stores all of the nodes found so far in the OPEN list and expands only one (the best) node located on the first position in the OPEN list. After generating and evaluating direct descendants/ ancestors (depending on the search direction) of the current node, the algorithm adds all of them into the OPEN list while at the same time performing an ordering of the list on the basis of the heuristic measures of the nodes (from a node with the best heuristic measure to the node with the worst heuristic measure).

glossary.definition: A data structure that implements the OPEN list, working according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7). The priority queue works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.

glossary.definition: a function used to distinguish clearly between nodes that give a win and nodes that give a defeat
glossary.sentence: It is also possible to use a heuristic evaluation function if it allows us to distinguish clearly between nodes that give a win and nodes that give a defeat; however, this is generally impractical

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of Hill Climbing where the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction).
glossary.sentence: the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction );

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of Hill Climbing where the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: Nodes generated from a current node in the backward direction of the search.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm expands the current node A by generating its three descendants—nodes D, H, and E.

glossary.definition: The number of best nodes examined during search, which is set before the algorithm starts searching.
glossary.sentence: The number of the best nodes examined during search is set before the algorithm starts searching. This number is called a beam width.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that selects a limited number of best nodes for further expansion in each iteration, providing a compromise between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search.
glossary.sentence: Beam Search is a compromise between Hill Climbing, which expands only one node per iteration, and Best-First Search, which stores and incrementally expands all nodes found before.

glossary.definition: a numerical value representing the estimated game state quality at a node, used to guide the search.
glossary.sentence: the heuristic measure of the current node

glossary.definition: a game where one of the players wins the game, and the other loses
glossary.sentence: If a game has two results (one of the players wins the game, and the other loses): +1 if a node corresponds to a win by the maximiser; −1 if a node corresponds to a win by the minimiser

glossary.definition: a game where there are three possible outcomes: a win, a draw, and a defeat
glossary.sentence: If a game has three results (a win, a draw, and a defeat): +1 if a node corresponds to a win by the maximiser; 0 if it is a draw; −1 if a node corresponds to a win by the minimiser

glossary.definition: a game tree structure where terminal nodes do not allow full evaluation of the game result, requiring heuristic approximation.
glossary.sentence: A partial state space graph is a more complex case, since the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.

glossary.definition: a game tree where all possible game states and outcomes are fully represented and evaluable.
glossary.sentence: Although we have applied the minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning to a complete state space graph in this game

glossary.definition: the final outcome of the game, represented as a numerical value derived from the evaluation of terminal nodes.
glossary.sentence: This is a game result

glossary.term: heuristic measure in partial state space graph
glossary.definition: a heuristic measure assigned to the node representing the current state of the game, which indicates only the best state that can be reached after making n moves from the current state
glossary.sentence: In the case of a partial state space graph, a heuristic measure assigned to the node representing the current state of the game indicates only the best state that can be reached after making n moves from the current state

glossary.definition: a path that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node while satisfying the following conditions: all nodes in the winning path are closely related; all nodes in the winning path have the same heuristic measures, which are equal to the game result
glossary.sentence: A winning path is a path that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node while satisfying the following conditions: all nodes in the winning path are closely related; all nodes in the winning path have the same heuristic measures, which are equal to the game result

glossary.term: paths leading to the best state
glossary.definition: paths that lead to the best state that can be obtained after making n moves from the current state, which are not winning paths
glossary.sentence: In the case of a partial state space graph, when the best state has been identified, paths leading to this state can be determined. These are not winning paths! These are paths leading to the best state that can be obtained after making n moves from the current state

glossary.definition: a decision-making algorithm used in game theory to minimize the possible loss for a worst-case scenario, used as a reference for comparison.
glossary.sentence: the same as provided by the minimax algorithm

glossary.definition: an alternative to the minimax algorithm that improves the search efficiency of the state space graph of the game by cutting off unpromising paths, while returning the same move as minimax and using the same propagation rules
glossary.sentence: Alpha-beta pruning improves the search efficiency by reducing the number of nodes evaluated and consequently the time spent searching for a solution. Let’s consider this approach. Alpha-beta pruning is an alternative to the minimax algorithm that improves the search efficiency of the state space graph of the game. It is based on the idea that moves that are not beneficial (or in other words, moves that make no difference to the final decision) can be ignored (Jones, 2009; Russell and Norvig, 2010). Alpha-beta pruning therefore returns the same move as minimax, but cuts off unpromising paths from the state space graph (Luger, 2009). As a result, it does not give heuristic measures for all nodes. This algorithm uses the same rules for propagating heuristic measures (Luger, 2009): if a node is located at MAX level, it acquires the maximum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants; if a node is located at MIN level, it acquires the minimum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants

glossary.definition: a value assigned to nodes at MAX levels, which should not decrease, as this would represent a bad move, and defines the best move that can be made to maximise the result of the game
glossary.sentence: An alpha number (α) is assigned to nodes at MAX levels. This value should not decrease, since this would represent a bad move. Hence, this number defines the best move that can be made to maximise the result of the game

glossary.definition: a value assigned to nodes at MIN levels, which should not increase, as this would represent a bad move, and defines the best move that can be done to minimise the result of the game
glossary.sentence: A beta number (β) is assigned to nodes at MIN levels. It should not increase, since this would represent a bad move. This number therefore defines the best move that can be done to minimise the result of the game

glossary.definition: a decision to cut off unconsidered paths below the current node when the β value of the current node is smaller than the α value of the ancestor.
glossary.sentence: an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node

glossary.definition: a decision to cut off unconsidered paths below the current node when the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor.
glossary.sentence: a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node

glossary.definition: a strategy for processing a partial state space graph by exploring the tree to a fixed depth N.
glossary.sentence: The two approaches of N-ply look-ahead and iterative deepening are used to process a partial state space graph

glossary.definition: a strategy for processing a partial state space graph that involves repeatedly applying a depth-limited search with increasing depth.
glossary.sentence: The two approaches of N-ply look-ahead and iterative deepening are used to process a partial state space graph

glossary.definition: a heuristic measure assigned during the algorithm's execution that is not yet final, used when a precise measure cannot be determined due to unconsidered descendants.
glossary.sentence: When the algorithm has assigned an intermediate heuristic measure, it checks whether the ancestor of the current node in the current path also has an intermediate heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: the intermediate heuristic measure of the current node, used in the context of α-cut-off.
glossary.sentence: the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor

glossary.definition: the intermediate heuristic measure of the ancestor node, used in the context of β-cut-off.
glossary.sentence: the β value of the ancestor is 1

glossary.definition: the action of eliminating unconsidered paths below a node to reduce computation.
glossary.sentence: the algorithm cuts off one unconsidered path

glossary.definition: a final, exact heuristic value assigned to a node when all descendants have been evaluated or when sufficient information is available.
glossary.sentence: the node whose unconsidered paths were cut off acquires a precise heuristic measure

glossary.definition: paths from a node that have not yet been evaluated or processed by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node

glossary.definition: the immediate child nodes of a given node in the tree.
glossary.sentence: the known heuristic measures of the direct descendants

glossary.definition: the depth or stage in the game tree, either MAX or MIN, determining the type of player (maximiser or minimiser) at that level.
glossary.sentence: the current node is located at the MIN level

glossary.definition: a level in the game tree where the player aims to maximize the game result.
glossary.sentence: the node is located at the MAX level

glossary.definition: a level in the game tree where the player aims to minimize the game result for the maximiser.
glossary.sentence: the current node is located at the MIN level

glossary.definition: paths in the game tree that lead to a favorable outcome for the player, identified using game results.
glossary.sentence: Using the game results, winning paths can be identified

glossary.definition: the measure of how effectively the algorithm reduces the number of nodes evaluated by cutting off unconsidered paths.
glossary.sentence: A more interesting issue is the efficiency of alpha-beta pruning.

glossary.definition: the topmost node in the game tree, from which the search begins.
glossary.sentence: there is no ancestor for the root node

glossary.definition: the player whose goal is to win the game and maximize the game result.
glossary.sentence: the task of the maximiser is to win the game

glossary.definition: the player whose goal is to minimize the game result for the maximiser, i.e., to prevent the maximiser from winning.
glossary.sentence: the task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser

glossary.definition: a traversal step in which the algorithm moves from a node to its parent in the path.
glossary.sentence: the algorithm goes up one level, via the same path that was used to go down

glossary.definition: a traversal step in which the algorithm moves from a node to one of its descendants in the path.
glossary.sentence: the algorithm goes down again, via the first unconsidered path

glossary.definition: the initial path from a node that has not yet been explored during the algorithm's execution.
glossary.sentence: the algorithm goes down again via the first unconsidered path

glossary.definition: to win the game by maximizing the game result.
glossary.sentence: the task of the maximiser is to win the game

glossary.definition: to minimize the game result for the maximiser, i.e., to prevent the maximizer.
a type of linear regression with a single input feature, which has a unique solution because the weights of the model can be calculated using specific equations
glossary.sentence: For univariate linear regression, there is a unique solution because the weights of the model can be calculated using the following equations (Russell and Norvig, 2010):

glossary.definition: a scenario in linear regression where more than one input variable is used to predict the output, making the calculation of weights non-trivial
glossary.sentence: However, calculating the weights for the case of multiple input features is not a trivial task.

glossary.definition: a mathematical expression that provides the exact solution to a problem, such as the solution for weights in linear regression, though it is not used in practice due to computational issues
glossary.sentence: There is also a closed-form solution like this one: However, this solution is not used in practice because the computation of matrices can fail if there is a problem of matrix singularity.

glossary.definition: a condition in matrix computation where the matrix cannot be inverted, leading to failure in solving equations
glossary.sentence: However, this solution is not used in practice because the computation of matrices can fail if there is a problem of matrix singularity.

glossary.definition: the amount of computational resources required to perform a task, such as the high cost of inverting a matrix product in linear regression
glossary.sentence: Moreover, the computational complexity of inverting a multiplication of a transpose of vector X and vector X is very high (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: a function that measures the difference between predicted and actual values, and calculating it for every possible weight value is inefficient for finding convergence
glossary.sentence: Additionally, calculating the loss function for every possible value of each weight over the entire dataset would be an inefficient way of finding the convergence point (Google Developers, n.d.).

glossary.definition: an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values, based on calculating partial derivatives with respect to all independent variables
glossary.sentence: Therefore, usually, another mechanism is used, which is called gradient descent. The gradient descent is an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values. The gradient descent is based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables.

glossary.definition: a function in logistic regression that needs to be minimised, similar to the cost function in linear regression
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression has the following cost function , which should also be minimised:

glossary.definition: a hyperparameter α used in gradient descent that represents the rate at which the linear regression updates the weights of the linear function, influencing the speed of convergence
glossary.sentence: To apply the gradient descent, a hyperparameter α called learning rate is used. It represents a rate at which Linear regression updates the weights of the linear function. Usually, the learning rate is associated with the algorithm’s speed of learning the underlying mathematical model or, in other words, how fast the algorithm converges to the error minima.

glossary.definition: a type of machine learning algorithm that uses labeled data to learn patterns, used for both regression and classification tasks
glossary.sentence: Summary of the main characteristics of the linear regression: It is a supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression.

glossary.definition: a machine learning task that involves predicting a continuous output variable based on input features
glossary.sentence: It is a supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression.

glossary.definition: an assumption in linear regression that the input variables and the output variable have a straight-line relationship
glossary.sentence: It assumes the linear relationship between the input variables (values of data object features) and the single output variable (a prediction).

glossary.definition: a common choice among activation functions
glossary.sentence: The four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: a performance evaluation metric in linear regression that accumulates the differences between predicted and real output values
glossary.sentence: The algorithm's performance is evaluated using the mean square error that accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.

glossary.definition: deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs
glossary.sentence: classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs

glossary.definition: a type of classification task when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive)
glossary.sentence: Binary classification is when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive);

glossary.definition: a type of classification task when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong
glossary.sentence: Multiclass classification is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong.

glossary.definition: a supervised learning algorithm used for classification tasks, particularly binary classification, that predicts one of two classes based on input features
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression , in its turn, is used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task. Therefore, it predicts one of two classes/categories to which a data object belongs to, for example, success/failure, good/bad, yes/no, win/loss, etc.

glossary.definition: a representation of input variables in linear regression, where independent variables are presented as a vector
glossary.sentence: In the case of Linear regression, the changing output is a value of the cost function across the whole dataset, input values are weight values of the linear function and independent variables are presented by a feature vector.

glossary.definition: a common choice among activation functions
glossary.sentence: The four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: a value, typically 0.5, used to decide the class label based on the output of the sigmoid function; if the value is greater than or equal to the threshold, the label is 1, otherwise 0
glossary.sentence: The threshold value of 0.5 at which the function crosses the y axis is used to decide about a class/category of a data object. If the squashed value is greater than the threshold value, the algorithm assigns a class label of 1 (success, good, yes, win, etc.), else it assigns a class label of 0 (failure, bad, no, loss, etc.)

glossary.definition: a machine learning algorithm previously discussed in the context of supervised learning, used for both regression and classification tasks
glossary.sentence: k-Nearest Neighbour ( kNN ) algorithm Previously you have learnt that supervised machine learning is used for regression and classification tasks. Linear regression is intended for the regression task while logistic regression – for the classification task.

glossary.definition: a unit of data that is clustered in the dataset
glossary.sentence: Therefore, each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster.

glossary.definition: a group of data objects that are similar to each other
glossary.sentence: Therefore, each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster.

glossary.definition: a clustering approach that starts with each data object as a separate cluster and recursively merges pairs of clusters at each level until one cluster with all data objects is acquired
glossary.sentence: Figure 2 represents the process of agglomerative hierarchical clustering in which clusters having the smallest distance between them are merged at each step of clustering.

glossary.definition: a clustering approach that starts with a single cluster containing all data objects and recursively splits clusters into two new clusters at each level until each data object forms its own cluster
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach. Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it.

glossary.definition: the measure of difference between two clusters
glossary.sentence: The pairs chosen for merging consist of the two groups with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity

glossary.definition: the measure of difference between two clusters after splitting
glossary.sentence: The split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity

glossary.definition: methods used to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods.

glossary.definition: a linkage method where the distance between clusters is calculated as the distance between the most distant elements of the two clusters
glossary.sentence: Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated

glossary.definition: a linkage method where the distance between clusters is calculated as the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated

glossary.definition: a linkage method where the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster

glossary.definition: a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: a branch in a dendrogram that represents the merging of data objects into clusters
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters

glossary.definition: the vertical position in a dendrogram indicating the level at which two data objects or clusters are merged
glossary.sentence: each clade has its height

glossary.definition: the terminal points of clades in a dendrogram that correspond to the data objects used in the clustering process
glossary.sentence: leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process

glossary.definition: a line drawn across a dendrogram to determine the number of clusters by counting the number of vertical lines it intersects
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.

glossary.definition: the result obtained by counting the number of vertical lines intersected by the horizontal cut-off line in a dendrogram
glossary.sentence: The number of vertical lines intersected by the horizontal cut- off line represents the number of clusters.

glossary.definition: tools used to assess the performance of a machine learning algorithm with different numbers of clusters
glossary.sentence: As a result, to make the right cut-off, the developers usually need to use evaluation metrics evaluating the algorithm's performance with the different number of clusters.

glossary.definition: a clustering method that creates a hierarchy of clusters through either merging (agglomerative) or splitting (divisive) processes
glossary.sentence: Summarising the information about the hierarchical clustering given in this topic, it is worth mentioning that the entire cluster hierarchy represents an ordered sequence of cluster merges

glossary.definition: the progression of cluster formation in hierarchical clustering where each step involves merging two clusters into one
glossary.sentence: the entire cluster hierarchy represents an ordered sequence of cluster merges (Hastie, 2017)

glossary.definition: a person who implements or decides on parameters of the clustering algorithm
glossary.sentence: Still, the developer needs to decide a) where to cut the dendrogram to receive the final number of clusters and b) which method of linkage to use to measure similarity between clusters.

glossary.definition: configurable values in a machine learning algorithm that are set before training and affect the model's behavior
glossary.sentence: Metrics depend on a task, whether it is classification, regression, or clustering.

glossary.definition: a type of machine learning problem such as classification, regression, or clustering
glossary.sentence: Metrics depend on a task, whether it is classification, regression, or clustering.

glossary.definition: a clustering algorithm that partitions data into K clusters based on distance to centroids
glossary.sentence: Pai (2021) indicates that one of the methods to implement this type of hierarchical clustering is to perform the procedure of K-Means recursively on each intermediate cluster till you encounter all the data objects in the dataset or the minimum number of data objects you desire to have in a cluster.

glossary.definition: a measure of dissimilarity between two data objects or clusters
glossary.sentence: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: a measure of how alike two data objects or clusters are, often inversely related to distance
glossary.sentence: Different linkage methods lead to different clusters, and thus the choice of the method depends on the developer.

glossary.definition: the body of research and studies focused on clustering methods and algorithms
glossary.sentence: According to (Hastie, 2017) , divisive hierarchical clustering has not been studied nearly as extensively as agglomerative clustering in the clustering literature.

glossary.definition: a cluster formed during the recursive process of clustering, used as input for further splitting or merging
glossary.sentence: Pai (2021) indicates that one of the methods to implement this type of hierarchical clustering is to perform the procedure of K-Means recursively on each intermediate cluster till you encounter all the data objects in the dataset or the minimum number of data objects you desire to have in a cluster.

glossary.definition: the smallest acceptable number of data objects in a cluster, used as a stopping condition in clustering
glossary.sentence: Pai (2021) indicates that one of the methods to implement this type of hierarchical clustering is to perform the procedure of K-Means recursively on each intermediate cluster till you encounter all the data objects in the dataset or the minimum number of data objects you desire to have in a cluster.

glossary.definition: the number of data objects in the dataset
glossary.sentence: attribute each data object to a single cluster. This step results in N clusters, where N is the number of data objects in the dataset

glossary.definition: a stage in the hierarchical clustering process where clusters are merged or split
glossary.sentence: Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster.

glossary.definition: a task of supervised machine learning that demands making predictions in a continuous range, with output being a real value
glossary.sentence: In general, the regression task demands making predictions in a continuous range. Thus, its output is a real value.

glossary.definition: a case in which the final output of the network can be compared with the desired (actual label) output
glossary.sentence: In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network.

glossary.definition: a metric that measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects, providing positive non-zero values
glossary.sentence: The Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects. It provides positive non-zero values.

glossary.definition: the same units of measurement as the quantity being estimated, but squared units; for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, the error will represent 'the squared number of persons'; if it predicts the house prices in EUR, then the error value will represent 'squared EUR'
glossary.sentence: This metric has the same units of measurement as the quantity being estimated. Still, they are squared units; for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, the error will represent "the squared number of persons"; if it predicts the house prices in EUR, then the error value will represent "squared EUR".

glossary.definition: the type of error is prone to outliers
glossary.sentence: This type of error is prone to outliers.

glossary.definition: a metric that applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable
glossary.sentence: Root mean square error (RMSE) applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable, for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, then the error will represent “the number of persons”; if it predicts the prices of the house in EUR, then the error value will represent “price in EUR”.

glossary.definition: a metric that is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values
glossary.sentence: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values.

glossary.definition: also known as the coefficient of determination; it indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset
glossary.sentence: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset.

glossary.definition: the coefficient values lie in a range [0,1], where 0 indicates that the model does not fit the data objects and 1 indicates the perfect fit
glossary.sentence: The coefficient values lie in a range [0,1], where 0 indicates that the model does not fit the data objects and 1 indicates the perfect fit.

glossary.definition: it represents to what extent the observed variance in the actual output values is explained by the features of the constructed model
glossary.sentence: It represents to what extent the observed variance in the actual output values is explained by the features of the constructed model, for example, if the R2 coefficient of a model is 0.88, then approximately 88% of the observed variation in the actual output values can be explained by the model’s features.

glossary.definition: a task that generally demands predicting a class/category to which a particular data object belongs
glossary.sentence: The classification task generally demands predicting a class/category to which a particular data object belongs.

glossary.definition: a typical approach in multiclass classification task in which each class is considered as a binary case, treating the class under consideration as a positive one while other classes are treated as negative ones
glossary.sentence: The typical approach in multiclass classification task is to consider each class as a binary case based on One-vs-Rest approaches in which the class under consideration is treated to be a positive one while other negative ones.

glossary.definition: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1); True negatives (TN) – a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0)
glossary.sentence: Correct predictions: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, and it is spam. True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0). For example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, and it is non-spam.

glossary.definition: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0)
glossary.sentence: Erroneous predictions: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as positive. False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as negative.

glossary.definition: a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category; it represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category
glossary.sentence: The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017). It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category (Russell & Norvig. 2010). Thus, it represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category.

glossary.term: structure of confusion matrix in binary classification
glossary.definition: each row represents an actual class, while each column represents a predicted class
glossary.sentence: Each row in a confusion matrix represents an actual class, while each column represents a predicted class (Geron, 2017).

glossary.term: main diagonal of confusion matrix in multiclass classification
glossary.definition: represents the number of data objects correctly predicted (true positives for a specific class/category)
glossary.sentence: In multiclass classification, the main diagonal of the confusion matrix represents the number of data objects correctly predicted (true positives for a specific class/category).

glossary.definition: values at the intersections of the column of the corresponding class with other classes
glossary.sentence: Taking a specific class/category, we can consider the values at the intersections of the column of the corresponding class with other classes as false positives

glossary.definition: values at the intersections of the row of the corresponding class with other classes
glossary.sentence: while values at the intersections of the row of the corresponding class with other classes as false negatives.

glossary.definition: other cells that do not intersect the column and row corresponding to a specific class/category
glossary.sentence: Other cells that do not intersect the column and row corresponding to a specific class/category represent true negatives for this specific class.

glossary.definition: evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.

glossary.definition: calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix
used in this topic to combine input signals
glossary.sentence: We will use the summation function further in this topic.

glossary.definition: determines the output of the neuron
glossary.sentence: The transfer (activation) function determines the output of the neuron.

glossary.definition: many activation functions have been tested, but only a few have found practical applications; the four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions
glossary.sentence: According to ( Negnevitsky , 2011), many activation functions have been tested, but only a few have found practical applications; the four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: a common choice among activation functions
glossary.sentence: The four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: a common choice among activation functions
glossary.sentence: The four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: the step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern
