glossary.sentence: Artificial neural network An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern (Callan, 2003; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: processing units or nodes
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes.

glossary.definition: single neurons
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes.

glossary.definition: single neurons
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes.

glossary.definition: transmitted between neurons
glossary.sentence: They are connected by transmitting signals to each other.

glossary.definition: produced by each neuron
glossary.sentence: You already know that each neuron produces an output.

glossary.definition: received from the environment by the input layer
glossary.sentence: The input layer does not contain computing neurons because it only receives inputs signals from the environment and passes them to the hidden layers.

glossary.definition: a typical structure of an artificial neural network
glossary.sentence: A typical artificial neural network comprises a hierarchy of layers, and the neurons on the networks are arranged along with these layers.

glossary.definition: the layers of neurons connected to the external environment
glossary.sentence: The neurons connected to the external environment form input and output layers.

glossary.definition: the layers of neurons connected to the external environment
glossary.sentence: The neurons connected to the external environment form input and output layers.

glossary.definition: the layers of neurons between input and output layers
glossary.sentence: The layers of neurons between input and output layers are called hidden layers.

glossary.definition: neurons in the output and hidden layers that apply summation and activation functions
glossary.sentence: On the other hand, the output and hidden layers contain computing neurons with summation and activation functions.

glossary.definition: all the neurons in a layer process their inputs simultaneously and independently
glossary.sentence: Moreover, artificial neural networks have a parallel processing nature because all the neurons in a layer process their inputs simultaneously and independently (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: a type of artificial neural network with connections only in one direction; every processing unit receives input from 'upstream' processing units and delivers output to 'downstream' processing units; there are no loops
glossary.sentence: A feed-forward network has connections only in one dir • ection . Every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units; there are no loops.

glossary.definition: a type of artificial neural network that feeds its outputs back into its inputs; the activation levels form a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour
glossary.sentence: A recurrent network , on the other hand, feeds its outputs back into its inputs. It means that the activation levels of the network form a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour.

glossary.definition: related to modifying weights in some particular way
glossary.sentence: Learning in artificial neural networks is related to modifying weights in some particular way .

glossary.definition: values that make the memory of the artificial neural network; represent a set of network's knowledge in a particular time moment
glossary.sentence: At the beginning of this topic, it was mentioned that weights make the memory of the artificial neural network. Therefore their values represent a set of network's knowledge in a particular time moment.

glossary.definition: applied to modify the weights of the network when there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network
glossary.sentence: In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network.

glossary.definition: resistance to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units; network operation changes very little
glossary.sentence: According to ( Borisovs et al., 1998), at the same time, artificial neural networks are resistant to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units and network operation changes very little. It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation

glossary.definition: resistance to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units; network operation changes very little
glossary.sentence: According to ( Borisovs et al., 1998), at the same time, artificial neural networks are resistant to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units and network operation changes very little. It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation

glossary.definition: the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network
glossary.sentence: It is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network ( Borisovs et al., 1998).

glossary.definition: projecting probable consequences of given situations
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems ((Waterman, 1986) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): Prediction - projecting probable consequences of given situations.

glossary.definition: finding the "best" organization of constraints
glossary.sentence: optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" organization of constraints

glossary.definition: separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal
glossary.sentence: noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal

glossary.definition: a system that stores a representation not only of facts but also of relations among them
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base stores a representation not only of facts but also of relations among them.

glossary.definition: a system that stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students
glossary.sentence: The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.

glossary.definition: a component that depends on a knowledge representation schema that is used to describe knowledge
glossary.sentence: The inference engine depends on a knowledge representation schema that is used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: a formal convention and rule used to describe knowledge
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: artificial intelligence methods that do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: artificial intelligence methods based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: intelligent computer systems that can be developed using weak methods, such as symbolic manipulation or mathematical calculations
glossary.sentence: Machine learning applications and search-based planning systems are examples of such systems.

glossary.definition: intelligent computer systems that can be developed using weak methods, such as symbolic manipulation or mathematical calculations
glossary.sentence: Machine learning applications and search-based planning systems are examples of such systems.

glossary.definition: the process of acquiring knowledge from a domain expert, processed by a specialist called a knowledge engineer and then added to the knowledge base
glossary.sentence: There are several main methods used for this purpose (Hopgood, 2001): 1) knowledge is acquired from a domain expert, processed by a specialist called a knowledge engineer and then added to the knowledge base, 2) the domain expert can describe and store knowledge using an automated knowledge acquisition system, and 3) nowadays intelligent computer systems are able to learn from examples.

glossary.definition: a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: a person who has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: knowledge that allows a domain expert to solve problems in a more effective way
glossary.sentence: Moreover, the domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: an intelligent computer system that emulates the human expert’s methodology and performance and is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems (Luger, 2009). An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: knowledge representation schemas that represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge
glossary.sentence: Logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge;

glossary.definition: knowledge representation schemas that represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them;

glossary.definition: knowledge representation schemas that also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/concept
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/concept;

glossary.term: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: knowledge representation schemas that represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions
glossary.sentence: Procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: theories that postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas are based on associationist theories of human mind that were developed in psychology. These theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts (Luger, 2009), for example, the concept “university” for the most part of students could be associated with such concepts as lectures, students, professors, exams, science etc. All the mentioned concepts define the meaning of the concept “university”.

glossary.definition: the way knowledge is stored at different hierarchical levels, where general features characterizing all birds are associated with a concept “Bird” and, as a result, to find an answer people need to move along the hierarchical path in the structure of the memory
glossary.sentence: Research works in psychology have revealed that concepts are not only related by associations but they are also organized in a hierarchical manner. In 1969, Collins and Quillian made an experiment with aim of understanding how people store knowledge. They asked people about birds and their features and recorded reaction time. The mean reaction time changed in relation to the complexity of a sentence. This proved that knowledge is stored at different hierarchical levels and time is needed to go along the path from the activated concept to its features. The results of the experiment are displayed in Figure 3. So, the previously described experiment showed that the mean reaction time to the question “Can a canary bird fly?” is higher than the mean reaction time to the question “Can a canary bird sing?” This is because people do not store all features of a specific bird, but they organize knowledge about birds in a hierarchical manner: general features characterizing all birds are associated with a concept “Bird” and, as a result, to find an answer people need to move along the hierarchical path in the structure of the memory.

glossary.definition: forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems ((Waterman, 1986) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.

glossary.definition: determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems ((Waterman, 1986) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.

glossary.definition: finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems ((Waterman, 1986) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.

glossary.definition: devising a sequence of the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the .
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems ((Waterman, 1986) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): Planning - devising a sequence of the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the .

glossary.definition: the process of observing and recording data over time to detect changes or anomalies
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems ((Waterman, 1986) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): Monitoring - the process of observing and recording data over time to detect changes or anomalies.

glossary.definition: the ability to make decisions quickly based on current data
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems ((Waterman, 1986) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): Real-time decision making - the ability to make decisions quickly based on current data.
